title: Another Game
description:
There are n heaps of coins and two players who move alternately. On each move, a player selects some of the nonempty heaps and removes one coin from each selected heap. The player who removes the last coin wins. For each test case, determine whether the first or second player wins if both play optimally.

Input
- First line: integer t, number of test cases
- For each test case:
  - Line 1: integer n
  - Line 2: n integers x_1, x_2, ..., x_n (heap sizes)

Output
- For each test case, print "first" if the first player wins, otherwise "second".

examples.explanation: Decide winner per test using the derived condition based on the largest heap and the total sum.

constraints: Sum of n over all tests ≤ 2⋅10^5

mcqChain.questionText: If there is only one heap with x coins, who wins (optimal play)?

options.label: First wins if x is odd, second wins if x is even
First wins if x is even, second wins if x is odd

mcqChain.explanation: Each move removes exactly one coin (only one heap exists), so the number of moves equals x. The last mover wins, determined by x's parity.

wrongExplanations.1: With one heap, parity matters; you cannot force extra moves.
wrongExplanations.3: Parity is reversed here; check simple cases x=1 and x=2.

mcqChain.hint: Test x=1 and x=2 to see the pattern.

mcqChain.questionText: On each move, a player may select any subset of nonempty heaps. What is the minimum possible number of moves until all heaps are empty (assuming players try to finish as fast as possible)?
mcqChain.diagram: Heaps: [x1, x2, ..., xn]. One coin per selected heap per move.
mcqChain.explanation: A heap can lose at most 1 coin per move. The largest heap needs max(x_i) moves; selecting all nonempty heaps each time achieves that bound.

wrongExplanations.1: sum(x_i) is the maximum number of moves if you remove from only one heap per move.
wrongExplanations.3: n is unrelated to how many moves needed; coin counts matter.

mcqChain.hint: Think about always choosing all nonempty heaps.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the maximum possible number of moves (if a player always selects exactly one heap per move)?
mcqChain.explanation: Removing from a single heap per move uses one move per coin; total coins are sum(x_i).

wrongExplanations.1: This is the minimum possible moves if you choose all heaps each time.
wrongExplanations.3: This is the total coins in other heaps, not the total moves.

mcqChain.hint: Each move removes one coin from one heap here.

mcqChain.questionText: Consider two heaps [a, b] with a ≥ b. If b = 0 (only one heap nonempty), who wins?

options.label: First wins if a is odd; otherwise second
It depends on b, not a

mcqChain.explanation: Only one heap remains. Each move removes one coin from that heap; parity of a decides the last mover.

wrongExplanations.1: Parity of a can make the second player take the last coin.
wrongExplanations.3: b=0, so only a matters.

mcqChain.hint: Reduce to the single-heap case.

mcqChain.questionText: Let s = sum(x_i), m = max(x_i). Which interval describes all possible numbers of moves T the game can last (depending on players' choices)?
mcqChain.explanation: You need at least m moves (largest heap), and at most s moves (one heap per move). Any T in between can be arranged.

wrongExplanations.2: 2m is arbitrary; upper bound is s.
wrongExplanations.3: n is unrelated to moves needed; coin counts bound T.

mcqChain.hint: Combine the minimum and maximum from previous questions.

mcqChain.questionText: Define r = s - m (total coins in the other heaps). If m > r, what must happen after at most r moves that exclude the largest heap?
mcqChain.diagram: Largest heap has m coins; other heaps have r coins total.

options.label: All other heaps become empty and only the largest heap remains, forcing the rest of the moves on it
The largest heap must be ignored for the rest of the game
Both players can always avoid touching the largest heap until the end

mcqChain.explanation: Only r coins exist outside the largest. After consuming them in at most r moves, only the largest heap remains and must be played until it reaches 0.

wrongExplanations.1: The game ends only when all heaps are 0; largest still has coins.
wrongExplanations.3: You cannot avoid the largest once other heaps are empty.

mcqChain.hint: Count how many moves can exclude the largest before others are gone.

mcqChain.questionText: If after other heaps are empty, the largest heap has L coins left and it's someone's turn, who wins this tail segment?
mcqChain.diagram: Tail: single heap with L coins; players alternate; last coin wins.

options.label: The player to move wins if L is odd; otherwise the next player (after them) wins
Always the player to move wins
Always the player not to move wins
Outcome depends on how many heaps existed before, not on L

mcqChain.explanation: Single-heap parity decides: odd L gives the current player the last coin, even L gives the opponent the last coin.

wrongExplanations.1: Parity matters in a single heap.
wrongExplanations.3: Only L matters once we're down to a single heap.

mcqChain.hint: Reduce to a single-heap parity case.

mcqChain.questionText: When m > r, why can the first player force a win regardless of the second player's choices?

options.label: Because after other heaps are exhausted, the remaining single-heap tail has odd length for the first player's turn sequence
Because the first player can remove 2 coins per move from the largest heap
Because the second player must skip moves
Because the total moves T equals sum(x_i) automatically

mcqChain.explanation: Other heaps can be drained in at most r moves; with m > r, the largest heap tail dominates. The first player can ensure the tail starts on their turn with favorable parity.

wrongExplanations.1: Each selected heap loses only 1 coin per move.
wrongExplanations.4: T can vary between m and s based on choices.

mcqChain.hint: Think about controlling when the tail (single largest heap) begins and its parity.

mcqChain.questionText: Consider heaps [5, 1, 1]. Here s=7, m=5, r=2. Who wins under optimal play?

options.label: Depends on the sequence of moves, not decidable

mcqChain.explanation: m(5) > r(2), so the game reduces to a tail on the largest heap that favors the first player.

wrongExplanations.1: With m > r, second cannot flip the outcome; tail parity favors first.
wrongExplanations.3: There is no tie; one player removes the last coin.

mcqChain.hint: Apply the m > r observation.

mcqChain.questionText: If m ≤ r (the largest heap is not greater than the sum of the others), players can often balance moves. What simple quantity then determines the winner?

options.label: Parity of s = sum(x_i): odd → first, even → second
Parity of the largest heap m only

mcqChain.explanation: When m ≤ r, players can pair removals across heaps to control the finish; the outcome depends on the overall parity of total coins s.

wrongExplanations.2: m alone does not decide when other heaps can be paired.
wrongExplanations.3: r’s parity alone is insufficient; total sum parity matters.

mcqChain.hint: Think about pairing coins across moves.

mcqChain.questionText: Evaluate heaps [2, 4]. s=6, m=4, r=2. Who wins?

options.label: Depends on the first move only

mcqChain.explanation: m(4) > r(2) is true, but parity of the tail and turn forces lead to an even total; using the general rule: s=6 is even and m ≤ r is false; however, combining choices yields second winning under optimal balancing.

wrongExplanations.1: Check the combined condition carefully; even total under optimal play can favor second.
wrongExplanations.3: There is no tie.

mcqChain.hint: Apply the conditions consistently across turns.

mcqChain.questionText: Pick the correct decision rule that determines the winner for any test case.

options.label: If m > s - m then first, else if s is odd then first, otherwise second
If m ≥ s - m then second, else if s is even then first, otherwise second
If s is odd then second, otherwise first
If m is odd then first, otherwise second

mcqChain.explanation: Largest vs rest decides when tail is forced; otherwise, parity of total coins decides.

wrongExplanations.1: The threshold is strict (>) and parity rule is reversed.
wrongExplanations.3: Largest heap parity alone is insufficient.

mcqChain.hint: Combine the m vs r observation with the sum parity logic.

mcqChain.questionText: Which quantities do we need to compute per test case to apply the rule efficiently?
mcqChain.explanation: We need s and m to compare m against s - m and to get parity of s.

wrongExplanations.1: min(x_i) does not impact the rule.
wrongExplanations.3: We also need the largest to compare against the rest.

mcqChain.hint: Look at the final rule inputs.

mcqChain.questionText: Given constraints (sum of n across tests ≤ 2⋅10^5), what per-test time is acceptable?
mcqChain.explanation: Summing and finding max are O(n), and the total across all tests is bounded.

wrongExplanations.2: We still need to scan all numbers to compute sum and max.
wrongExplanations.3: You cannot avoid reading and processing all heap sizes.

mcqChain.hint: Reading and processing each heap exactly once suffices.

mcqChain.questionText: Choose the correct import for fast input/output in Python.
mcqChain.explanation: We will use sys.stdin.buffer and sys.stdout for efficiency. Other imports are unnecessary here.

wrongExplanations.3: Not needed unless using custom I/O wrappers.

mcqChain.hint: We need only standard sys for buffer I/O.

mcqChain.questionText: Start the solver structure.
mcqChain.explanation: We'll define solve() and call it under the main guard.

wrongExplanations.1: No need for args; we read from stdin.
wrongExplanations.3: Arbitrary names work, but we'll use solve() consistently.

mcqChain.hint: Use a simple function name.

mcqChain.questionText: Efficiently read all input tokens for large t and n.
mcqChain.explanation: Reading the whole buffer and splitting tokens is fast and simple for many integers.

wrongExplanations.2: input() in a loop is slower and may TLE.
wrongExplanations.3: You need all tokens, not a single line.

mcqChain.hint: Favor buffer-based batch reading.

mcqChain.questionText: Create an iterator over tokens and read t.
mcqChain.explanation: Converting the first token to int gives t; iterating avoids manual indexing.

wrongExplanations.3: len(data) is number of tokens, not t.

mcqChain.hint: Use next(it) to consume tokens in order.

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize output collection and loop over test cases.
mcqChain.explanation: Collect outputs to write once; loop over range(t).

wrongExplanations.1: Printing inside the loop is fine but we'll buffer outputs.
wrongExplanations.3: t is an int, not iterable.

mcqChain.hint: Use a list and a for-range loop.

mcqChain.questionText: Read n and the n heap sizes for each test case.

options.label: n = int(next(it)); arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]
arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]; n = int(next(it))

mcqChain.explanation: Consume n, then read exactly n integers using next(it).

wrongExplanations.1: next(it) returns a single token, not a line; mapping it won't iterate over more tokens.
wrongExplanations.3: Order matters; must read n first.

mcqChain.hint: Use a comprehension consuming n tokens.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
n = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(n)]

mcqChain.questionText: Compute s, m, and decide the winner using the rule.
mcqChain.explanation: We need s=sum(arr), m=max(arr), and the strict '>' plus odd-s check. Append the corresponding result.

wrongExplanations.1: The threshold must be strict '>' and parity condition should be for odd s.
wrongExplanations.2: s should be sum, not the length.
wrongExplanations.3: We compare max against the rest; min is irrelevant.

mcqChain.hint: Careful with '>' vs '>=' and which parity triggers 'first'.

mcqChain.questionText: Write buffered output at the end and add the main guard.
mcqChain.explanation: Join with newlines and write once. The main guard ensures solve() runs when executed.

wrongExplanations.1: Printing the list shows brackets; we need lines.
wrongExplanations.3: Writing after solve() but outside the function won't have 'out_lines' in scope.

mcqChain.hint: Ensure 'out_lines' is written inside solve() and main calls solve().

mcqChain.questionText: Test the rule on heaps [1, 1, 2]: s=4, m=2, r=2. What does the program print?
mcqChain.explanation: m(2) ≯ r(2), and s is even, so it prints "second".

wrongExplanations.3: Order doesn't affect sum/max.

mcqChain.hint: Apply the exact rule step by step.

mcqChain.questionText: For heaps [7, 1, 1, 1], s=10, m=7, r=3. What is the output?

options.label: It alternates: first, second, first, second

mcqChain.explanation: m(7) > r(3), so the rule gives "first".

wrongExplanations.1: Largest dominates; second cannot avoid the tail.
wrongExplanations.3: Outcome is well-defined under optimal play.

mcqChain.hint: Check m > r first.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity per test case of computing s and m and printing the result?
mcqChain.explanation: We scan the n numbers once to compute sum and max: O(n).

wrongExplanations.2: We cannot compute sum/max without reading all numbers.
wrongExplanations.3: No quadratic operations are needed.

mcqChain.hint: Each heap is processed once.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the overall time complexity across all tests, given that the sum of n over tests is at most 2⋅10^5?
mcqChain.explanation: We process each element once overall, so linear in total input size.

wrongExplanations.2: n varies per test; total linear work sums over all tests.

mcqChain.hint: Sum the per-test O(n) over all tests.

mcqChain.questionText: Which edge case must be handled explicitly when computing m and s?

options.label: n can be 1; max(arr) and sum(arr) still work correctly
Heaps can be negative; take absolute values
We must sort the array before computing sum and max

mcqChain.explanation: With n=1, sum and max are well-defined. All x_i ≥ 1 by constraints; no need to sort.

wrongExplanations.3: Sorting is unnecessary; sum and max don't require it.

mcqChain.hint: Check constraints for valid x_i.

reasoningChain.question: What happens in a single-heap game, and how does parity determine the winner?
reasoningChain.insight: With one heap, each move removes exactly one coin; the last mover wins iff the heap size is odd.

reasoningChain.question: What are the bounds on the number of moves T in the multi-heap game?
reasoningChain.insight: T lies between max(x_i) (if you always take from all nonempty heaps) and sum(x_i) (if you take from one heap per move).

reasoningChain.question: How does comparing the largest heap m to the sum of the others r = s - m affect the endgame?
reasoningChain.insight: If m > r, after at most r moves that exclude the largest, only the largest remains, forcing a tail segment whose parity the first player can make favorable.

reasoningChain.question: What determines the winner when m ≤ r?
reasoningChain.insight: Players can balance removals across heaps, and the outcome reduces to parity of the total sum s: odd → first, even → second.

reasoningChain.question: Which quantities must be computed per test, and how to implement efficiently?
reasoningChain.insight: Compute s and m in O(n) using a scan; decide via a simple condition; handle many tests with buffered I/O.

reasoningChain.question: How to assemble a robust Python implementation?
reasoningChain.insight: Use sys.stdin.buffer to read all tokens, iterate, compute s and m, apply rule, buffer outputs, and print once.
